title: "Ragged Edge"
featuredImage: "https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/IMG_0552.jpg"
classRating: "class5"
ydsRating: "7"
ydsSubRating: "none"
pitches: 6
parentArea: content/areas/vesper-peak.mdx
calTopoUrl: "https://caltopo.com/m/CD5M"
gpxFile: "593"
---

Gorgeous views and a great climb!

![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/21740765_10214426013466404_703195198429727251_o-1024x462.jpg)

## Resources

- [Steph Abegg](http://www.stephabegg.com/home/tripreports/washington/northcascades/vesper3)

## Gear

- 60m rope
- Doubles of 0.3 - 1, singles of 2, 3
- Small nuts
- 12 quickdraws

## Approach

The climb is accessed by a ledge system that cuts across the north face of Vesper at about 5800 ft elevation and begins at a small notch overlooking the Vesper Glacier.

Walk out the ledge on steep exposed heather (snow until mid-late summer?). When you can't walk any further either (A) scramble up over an obvious chockstone formed by a large, thin flake to a belay ledge or (B) as a variation backtrack a bit and figure out an exposed 3rd class traverse down and around the toe of a buttress before scrambling back up to an obvious and clean 5.6 layback crack (better start). The 3rd class slabs at mid-height on the first pitch could easily be accessed after climbing the lower half of the north face as well. 

Mountain Project

## Climb

/>

## Pitch 1

Climb approximately 60 ft of low-5th terrain to 3rd class slabs. Continue up the obvious flaky gully and arrange a gear belay just below a short overhanging wall (low-5th 170 ft). 

Mountain Project

![The start of P1](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/117533174_large_15646780621-793x1024.jpg)

I believe this photo is actually only halfway up P1, and that's where we went left off of both of the official routes...

<RouteOverlay 
  imageSrc="https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/IMG_7621-1024x683.jpg"

P3&quot;,&quot;ta&quot;:&quot;l&quot;,&quot;tb&quot;:false},{&quot;it&quot;:0,&quot;is&quot;:4.285714285714286,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;lw&quot;:3,&quot;cp&quot;:[{&quot;x&quot;:1352,&quot;y&quot;:458},{&quot;x&quot;:1168,&quot;y&quot;:418}],&quot;nl&quot;:10,&quot;fl&quot;:10,&quot;lc&quot;:false}]}"
  topoOverlaySrc="data:image/png;base64,"
/>

## Pitch 2

Traverse right on a long, thin ledge then a short gully to a fixed belay on the skyline (4th 60 ft). 

Mountain Project

The following picture is the INCORRECT pitch 2 of our previous trip

![Alexis at anchor #1, we went up that slanted crack](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/21728700_10214426006426228_4514736589168827609_o-1024x683.jpg)

## Alternative 1-2

**Slightly contrived variation start with better climbing - Blue Line**

**P1)**&nbsp;Climb a nice layback flake then a low-angle groove to 3rd class slabs. Traverse hard right then follow the highest grassy ledge system approximately 40 ft to a gear belay below a faint white dyke splitting a slab (5.6 160 ft). Note that you can also reach this belay from the original start as well.

**P2)**&nbsp;Climb the dyke past three bolts to a thin ledge. Traverse right and up a short gully to a fixed anchor on the skyline (5.7 90 ft). 

Mountain Project

## Pitch 3

Step right then traverse back left on positive edges towards the skyline. Find a bolt then continue up a nice arête protected by fixed pins. Arrange a gear belay atop a heather ledge (5.7 95 ft). 

Mountain Project

![An old piton on Pitch 3 of the route. This pitch goes up the seam in the photo, and there are enough features to make it 5.7. -Steph Abegg](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/27822784956_e60d895f03_b1-1024x768.jpg)

![Looking down P3 from the belay](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/111137930_large_14943410681-768x1024.jpg)

## Pitch 4

One person says it's a good fun pitch

Step right and climb steep, stacked blocks. Easy zig-zag cracks above lead to a fixed anchor. I aggressively trundled loose and semi-loose blocks off this pitch but some caution is still advised (5.7 95 ft). 

Mountain Project

![The zig zag cracks (5.7) of Pitch 4.](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/27579332540_a6481589e9_b1-768x1024.jpg)

<RouteOverlay 
  imageSrc="https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/27822784066_04b19bcc0b_b1-1024x768.jpg" 
  topoData="{&quot;items&quot;:[{&quot;it&quot;:5,&quot;is&quot;:1.4628571428571429,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;x&quot;:410,&quot;y&quot;:162,&quot;t&quot;:&quot;Top of P4\nanchor&quot;,&quot;ta&quot;:&quot;l&quot;,&quot;tb&quot;:false},{&quot;it&quot;:0,&quot;is&quot;:1.4628571428571429,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;lw&quot;:3,&quot;cp&quot;:[{&quot;x&quot;:570,&quot;y&quot;:169},{&quot;x&quot;:623,&quot;y&quot;:158},{&quot;x&quot;:682,&quot;y&quot;:159}],&quot;nl&quot;:10,&quot;fl&quot;:10,&quot;lc&quot;:false},{&quot;it&quot;:5,&quot;is&quot;:1.4628571428571429,&quot;ic&quot;:&quot;#ffff00&quot;,&quot;ia&quot;:1,&quot;x&quot;:581,&quot;y&quot;:137,&quot;t&quot;:&quot;P5&quot;,&quot;ta&quot;:&quot;l&quot;,&quot;tb&quot;:false}]}"
  topoOverlaySrc="data:image/png;base64,"
/>

## Pitch 5

Step right again and climb straight up in an exposed position. At the second bolt traverse right 50 ft to a fixed anchor on the skyline (5.7 80 ft). 

Mountain Project

- ![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/21688146_10214426012026368_2458600860079125341_o-1024x683.jpg) Alexis over at anchor #5, second to last pitch. It does quite a bit of traversing!
- ![](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/21688075_10214426009626308_2399381760872806715_o-1024x683.jpg) Alexis leaving anchor #5 and climbing the second to last pitch. I really did a lot of zig zagging protecting that route. Didn't want to take a fall there since there's an edge beneath it that you would fall over.

## Pitch 6

Mossy and a bit runout

Follow the nice arête to a final touch of heather and the summit. (5.5 130 ft). Walk off to the southeast. 

Mountain Project

![,From the top, the final last section!](https://andrewbares.blob.core.windows.net/hiking-blog-images/2019/08/21740830_10214426013746411_7391383789814453542_o-935x1024.jpg)
